name: Carrie Brown
summary: Carrie Brown, a prostitute known as 'Old Shakespeare,' was found murdered and mutilated in her New York City hotel room. She was initially suspected as a Jack the Ripper copycat victim but the timing didn't align. Ameer Ben Ali was convicted but pardoned after 11 years. The actual killer was never conclusively identified.
lastSeen: April 24, 1891, East River Hotel, New York City

name: Andrew & Abby Borden
summary: Andrew Borden and his wife Abby were found hacked to death in their Fall River, Massachusetts home. Their daughter Lizzie was acquitted of the murders. The case became one of the most famous unsolved murders in American history and spawned decades of debate, books, and plays.
lastSeen: August 4, 1892, 92 Second St., Fall River, MA

name: Stanford White
summary: Prominent architect Stanford White was shot in front of hundreds of witnesses at Madison Square Garden's rooftop restaurant by millionaire Harry K. Thaw, who was enraged over White's past relationship with his wife Evelyn Nesbit. Thaw was found not guilty by reason of insanity and confined to an asylum. The case was dubbed 'The Trial of the Century' of its era.
lastSeen: June 25, 1906, Madison Square Garden, New York City

name: Virginia Christian
summary: Seventeen-year-old Virginia Christian became the first woman executed in Virginia's electric chair for the murder of her employer. The case raised questions about juvenile justice and racial disparities. She was executed in August 1912.

name: New Orleans Axeman Victims
summary: The New Orleans Axeman murdered at least eight people and attacked several others between 1918 and 1919. He sent a letter to newspapers saying he would spare any household playing jazz music on a particular night. The killer was never identified. The case remains one of America's most famous historical unsolved serial murder cases.

name: Virginia Rappe
summary: Silent film actress Virginia Rappe died following a party at the St. Francis Hotel in San Francisco. Actor Roscoe 'Fatty' Arbuckle was charged with her rape and murder three times; all trials ended in acquittal or hung jury. The actual cause of death remains disputed between a bladder rupture or assault.
lastSeen: September 5, 1921, Hotel St. Francis, San Francisco, CA

name: William Desmond Taylor
summary: Silent film director William Desmond Taylor was found shot in his bungalow in Los Angeles. The murder implicated several silent film actresses, including Mabel Normand and Mary Miles Minter. Suspects were never prosecuted and the case became one of Hollywood's oldest unsolved murders.
lastSeen: February 1, 1922, his bungalow in Los Angeles, CA

name: Bobby Franks
summary: Fourteen-year-old Bobby Franks was murdered by wealthy University of Chicago students Nathan Leopold and Richard Loeb as a thrill kill. Attorney Clarence Darrow's defense saved them from execution; they received life sentences. Loeb was killed in prison; Leopold was eventually paroled in 1958.
lastSeen: May 21, 1924, near his home in Kenwood, Chicago

name: Judge Joseph Force Crater
summary: New York Supreme Court Justice Joseph Force Crater disappeared in New York City after dining with friends. He hailed a cab and was never seen again. Despite massive investigation, no trace of him was ever found. He was declared legally dead in 1939 and the mystery became a cultural touchstone for unexplained disappearances.
lastSeen: August 6, 1930, West 45th Street, New York City

name: Ruby Bates & Victoria Price
summary: The Scottsboro Boys case involved nine Black teenagers falsely accused of raping two white women on a train in Alabama. Multiple boys were wrongfully convicted despite recanted accusations. The case became a landmark in civil rights and legal history, with the Supreme Court overturning convictions twice.
lastSeen: March 25, 1931, freight train near Scottsboro, AL

name: Starr Faithfull
summary: Starr Faithfull was found drowned on a Long Beach, New York beach. Her diary revealed an ongoing relationship with politician Andrew James Peters who allegedly abused her. The death was undetermined — possibly homicide, suicide, or accident. The case inspired multiple novels and continues to be debated.
lastSeen: June 8, 1931, beach in Long Beach, NY

name: Charles Lindbergh Jr.
summary: The infant son of aviator Charles Lindbergh was kidnapped from the family estate in Hopewell, New Jersey. A ransom was paid but the baby's body was found two months later. Bruno Richard Hauptmann was convicted and executed in 1936. Some historians have questioned whether Hauptmann acted alone or was innocent.
lastSeen: March 1, 1932, family estate in Hopewell, NJ

name: Paul Bern
summary: MGM film director Paul Bern was found shot at his Beverly Hills home. It was ruled a suicide but a note was found that some interpret as a murder. Theories point to Dorothy Millette, his common-law wife who was found drowned days later. MGM allegedly covered up details of the investigation.
lastSeen: September 5, 1932, his home in Beverly Hills, CA

name: Cleveland Torso Murders
summary: The Cleveland Torso Murderer killed at least 12 people in the Kingsbury Run neighborhood of Cleveland between 1934 and 1938. Victims were decapitated and dismembered; most were never identified. Despite legendary detective Eliot Ness leading the investigation, the killer was never caught.

name: Thelma Todd
summary: Actress Thelma Todd was found dead in her garage from carbon monoxide. The death was ruled accidental but many believed it was murder. Theories pointed to her business partner Roland West or mob figure Lucky Luciano. The case was never conclusively resolved.
lastSeen: December 16, 1935, Thelma Todd's Sidewalk Café, Pacific Palisades, CA

name: Amelia Earhart
summary: Pioneer aviator Amelia Earhart and navigator Fred Noonan vanished over the Pacific Ocean during her attempt to circumnavigate the globe. She was declared legally dead in 1939. Multiple theories exist including the crash-and-sink theory and the Gardner Island castaway theory. Her fate remains one of aviation's greatest mysteries.
lastSeen: July 2, 1937, somewhere over the central Pacific Ocean

name: Betty June Binnicker & Mary Emma Thames
summary: Two young girls were found murdered in Alcolu, South Carolina. George Stinney Jr., a 14-year-old Black boy, was arrested, tried, and executed in 83 days — the youngest person executed in the 20th century US. In 2014, a judge vacated his conviction, ruling his trial was fundamentally unfair. The actual killer was never identified.

name: Recy Taylor
summary: Recy Taylor, a young Black woman, was gang-raped by six white men while walking home from church in Abbeville, Alabama. Two grand juries refused to indict the perpetrators. Rosa Parks investigated the case for the NAACP. Taylor lived to see renewed public attention before her death in 2017. No one was ever prosecuted.

name: Suzanne Degnan
summary: Six-year-old Suzanne Degnan was abducted from her bedroom in Chicago and dismembered. William Heirens, known as the 'Lipstick Killer' for writing on walls with lipstick, was convicted in 1946 and spent 65 years in prison. He consistently maintained innocence until his death in 2012, and many experts questioned the evidence.
lastSeen: January 7, 1946, family home in Chicago, IL

name: Texarkana Moonlight Murders
summary: The Phantom Killer committed five known murders and two attacks in the Texarkana area across the Texas-Arkansas border in 1946. The victims were typically couples in cars on lover's lanes. Despite a massive manhunt, the killer was never identified. The case inspired multiple films including 'The Town That Dreaded Sundown.'

name: Elizabeth Short (The Black Dahlia)
summary: The mutilated body of 22-year-old Elizabeth Short was found in a vacant lot in Los Angeles. Her body had been severed at the waist, drained of blood, and posed. The case became one of the most famous unsolved murders in American history, generating hundreds of confessions but no confirmed suspect.
lastSeen: January 9, 1947, at the Biltmore Hotel, Los Angeles

name: Dorothy Forstein
summary: Dorothy Forstein, wife of Philadelphia magistrate Jules Forstein, disappeared from her home in the middle of the night. Her children slept through the abduction. She had survived a vicious attack in her home years earlier. Despite investigation, she was never found and the case remains one of Philadelphia's most mysterious disappearances.
lastSeen: October 18, 1950, family home in Philadelphia, PA

name: Beverly Potts
summary: Ten-year-old Beverly Potts disappeared from a Cleveland park while attending a neighborhood show. Despite one of the largest manhunts in Cleveland history, she was never found. The case remained one of Cleveland's most haunting cold cases for over 70 years.

name: Bobby Greenlease
summary: Six-year-old Bobby Greenlease was kidnapped from his school and killed by Carl Austin Hall and Bonnie Brown Heady in Kansas City. A ransom of $600,000 was paid — the largest at that time. Hall and Heady were arrested, tried, and executed in less than three months. Half the ransom was never recovered.
lastSeen: September 28, 1953, Notre Dame de Sion School, Kansas City, MO

name: Evelyn Hartley
summary: Fifteen-year-old Evelyn Hartley disappeared while babysitting in La Crosse, Wisconsin. Blood and her shoe were found at the scene. Despite a massive investigation, she was never found. Her case remains one of Wisconsin's oldest open missing persons cases.
lastSeen: October 24, 1953, babysitting job in La Crosse, WI

name: Frank Olson
summary: CIA scientist Frank Olson died after falling from a New York hotel window. It was ruled a suicide but his family believed he was killed because he wanted to leave the agency's secret mind control program MKUltra. His body was exhumed in 1994 and a second autopsy suggested possible homicide.
lastSeen: November 28, 1953, Hotel Statler, New York City

name: Janet Christman
summary: Thirteen-year-old Janet Christman was babysitting when she was raped and strangled in Columbia, Missouri. Her body was found by the parents when they returned home. Despite the fact that a partial fingerprint was recovered, the case was never solved. It is Missouri's oldest open murder case.
lastSeen: March 18, 1953, babysitting job in Columbia, MO

name: Marilyn Sheppard
summary: Marilyn Sheppard was bludgeoned to death in her Bay Village, Ohio home. Her husband, Dr. Sam Sheppard, was convicted of murder in 1954 but his conviction was overturned. Retried in 1966, he was acquitted. His case inspired the TV show and film 'The Fugitive.' The actual killer was never conclusively identified.
lastSeen: July 4, 1954, family home in Bay Village, OH

name: Emmett Till
summary: Fourteen-year-old Emmett Till, a Black teenager from Chicago, was brutally murdered in Money, Mississippi after allegedly whistling at a white woman. His killers Roy Bryant and J.W. Milam were acquitted by an all-white jury in September 1955. They later confessed to the murder in a magazine interview, immune from prosecution. The case became a catalyst for the Civil Rights Movement.
lastSeen: August 24, 1955, Bryant's Grocery Store, Money, MS

name: Emmett Till Reexamination (2022)
summary: In 2022, the Department of Justice reopened the Emmett Till murder case after a manuscript revealed that Carolyn Bryant Donham had lied during the 1955 trial. She died in 2023 before any charges could be filed. The case remains officially unsolved as the original killers and Donham never faced justice.
lastSeen: August 24, 1955, Bryant's Grocery Store, Money, MS

name: Barbara & Patricia Grimes
summary: Sisters Barbara (15) and Patricia (13) Grimes disappeared after seeing an Elvis Presley movie in Chicago. Their nude bodies were found in a ditch outside the city weeks later. Despite extensive investigation and multiple suspects, including a confession later recanted, no one was ever convicted of their murders.

name: Daniel Barter
summary: Four-year-old Daniel Barter disappeared from his family home near Perdido Bay, Alabama on June 18, 1959. Despite searches, he was never found. The FBI listed his case as one needing fresh leads. After more than 60 years, the case remains open and Daniel's fate is unknown.
lastSeen: June 18, 1959, family home near Perdido Bay, AL

name: Henry Lee Lucas Victims
summary: Henry Lee Lucas confessed to hundreds of murders but most were unverifiable. He was confirmed in several cases including the murder of his mother and with accomplice Ottis Toole. A Texas task force eventually cleared him of all but a few confessed murders. He died in prison in 2001.

name: John Kinne
summary: John Kinne was found shot in his Kansas City home. His wife Sharon was charged and tried three times, resulting in hung juries. She later murdered a woman in Mexico and fled. The case against her for John's murder was never successfully prosecuted.
lastSeen: March 19, 1960, family home in Kansas City, MO

name: Boston Strangler Victims
summary: Thirteen women were strangled in the Boston area between 1962 and 1964. Albert DeSalvo confessed to all 13 murders but was never tried for them. DNA evidence collected in 2013 linked DeSalvo to the last victim, Mary Sullivan. Questions remain about whether DeSalvo committed all the murders.

name: William 'Billy' Jones Jr.
summary: Three-year-old William 'Billy' Jones Jr. disappeared from Vineland, New Jersey on December 17, 1962. Despite investigation, he was never found. His case is one of the oldest open missing person cases listed by the FBI.
lastSeen: December 17, 1962, near his home in Vineland, NJ

name: 16th Street Baptist Church Bombing Victims
summary: A KKK bomb killed four young Black girls — Addie Mae Collins, Cynthia Wesley, Carole Robertson, and Carol Denise McNair — at the 16th Street Baptist Church in Birmingham, Alabama. Robert Chambliss was convicted in 1977. Thomas Blanton Jr. and Bobby Frank Cherry were convicted in 2001 and 2002 respectively.
lastSeen: September 15, 1963, 16th Street Baptist Church, Birmingham, AL

name: Frank Sinatra Jr. Kidnapping
summary: Frank Sinatra Jr. was kidnapped from his hotel room at Harrah's Lake Tahoe on December 8, 1963. A ransom of $240,000 was paid. He was released after three days. The kidnappers Barry Keenan, Joe Amsler, and John Irwin were caught and convicted. Sinatra Jr. was unharmed.
lastSeen: December 8, 1963, Harrah's Hotel, Lake Tahoe, NV

name: John F. Kennedy Assassination
summary: President John F. Kennedy was shot and killed while riding in a presidential motorcade through Dealey Plaza in Dallas. Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested as a suspect but was shot and killed by Jack Ruby two days later before trial. The Warren Commission concluded Oswald acted alone, but debate continues. It remains officially unsolved.

name: Medgar Evers
summary: Civil rights leader Medgar Evers was shot in the driveway of his Jackson, Mississippi home. White supremacist Byron De La Beckwith was tried twice in 1964 but both trials ended in hung juries. He was finally convicted of the murder in 1994, more than 30 years after the crime.
lastSeen: June 12, 1963, his home in Jackson, MS

name: James Chaney, Andrew Goodman & Michael Schwerner
summary: Three civil rights workers disappeared in Neshoba County, Mississippi while investigating the burning of a Black church. Their bodies were found 44 days later. Edgar Ray Killen was convicted of manslaughter in 2005, four decades after the murders. The case became a landmark of the Civil Rights era.
lastSeen: June 21, 1964, County Road 515, Neshoba County, MS

name: Kitty Genovese
summary: Kitty Genovese was stabbed to death outside her apartment in Kew Gardens, Queens. The case became famous for the 'bystander effect' theory after news reports claimed 38 neighbors heard or witnessed the attack without calling police. Winston Moseley was convicted and died in prison in 2016. The bystander effect narrative was later found to be greatly exaggerated.
lastSeen: March 13, 1964, Austin Street, Kew Gardens, Queens

name: Alice Crimmins
summary: The two young children of Alice Crimmins, Eddie Jr. (5) and Missy (4), were found dead in Queens, New York. Alice Crimmins was convicted of manslaughter in 1968 in a sensational trial that was heavily influenced by her social life and behavior rather than direct evidence. The convictions were controversial and the case remains debated.
lastSeen: July 13, 1965, family apartment in Kew Gardens Hills, Queens

name: Dorothy Kilgallen
summary: Celebrity journalist Dorothy Kilgallen was found dead of an overdose of alcohol and barbiturates in her New York townhouse. She had been publicly investigating the JFK assassination and the Jack Ruby murder trial. Some researchers suspect foul play, though the death was officially ruled accidental.
lastSeen: November 7, 1965, her home in New York City

name: Jimmie Lee Jackson
summary: Jimmie Lee Jackson was a Black civil rights marcher shot by Alabama State Trooper James Bonard Fowler during a night march in Marion. His death inspired the Selma-to-Montgomery marches and the Voting Rights Act. Fowler was not charged until 2007 and pleaded guilty to misdemeanor manslaughter in 2010.

name: Cheri Jo Bates
summary: Cheri Jo Bates was found stabbed to death near the library of Riverside City College. The Zodiac Killer sent letters claiming responsibility for the murder, though investigators debated whether it was truly his work. The case remains unsolved and Bates is often included in discussions of Zodiac victims.
lastSeen: October 30, 1966, Riverside City College Library, CA

name: Richard Speck Victims
summary: Richard Speck broke into a Chicago townhouse and methodically murdered eight student nurses. Corazon Amurao survived by hiding under a bed and later identified Speck. He was convicted and sentenced to death, later commuted to 400 years. He died in prison in 1991.

name: Valerie Percy
summary: Valerie Percy, daughter of Senate candidate Charles Percy, was stabbed and beaten to death in her family's lakeside mansion in Kenilworth. A mysterious intruder entered through a window. Despite federal and local investigation, no suspect was ever charged. The case was one of Illinois's highest-profile unsolved murders.
lastSeen: September 17, 1966, family home in Kenilworth, IL

name: Rowe Family Massacre
summary: Eight members of the Rowe family were murdered in Pike County, Kentucky in January 1967. The case shocked the Appalachian community. The case was eventually linked to a local family feud that had persisted for generations.
lastSeen: January 11, 1967, family home in Pike County, KY

name: Barbara Jane Mackle
summary: Barbara Jane Mackle, daughter of a wealthy developer, was kidnapped from an Emory University motel room and buried alive in a ventilated box. Her captors Gary Steven Krist and Ruth Eisemann-Schier demanded ransom. She was found alive after 83 hours. Both were convicted.
lastSeen: December 17, 1968, motel near Emory University, GA

name: David Faraday & Betty Lou Jensen
summary: David Faraday (17) and Betty Lou Jensen (16) were the Zodiac Killer's first confirmed victims, shot on a dark lover's lane in Benicia, California. Faraday was shot in the head as he got out of his car; Jensen was shot while running. The Zodiac later claimed this murder in letters to police.
lastSeen: December 20, 1968, Lake Herman Road, Benicia, CA

name: Jane Durrua
summary: Thirteen-year-old Jane Durrua was abducted and murdered in Rahway, New Jersey. Robert Zarinsky was convicted of her murder in 2001 using genetic evidence, 33 years after the crime. He was also linked to additional murders. The case was an early example of cold-case DNA exoneration methodology.
lastSeen: October 28, 1968, her school in Rahway, NJ

name: Robert F. Kennedy Assassination
summary: Senator Robert F. Kennedy was shot at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles after winning the California presidential primary. Sirhan Sirhan was convicted and imprisoned. However, there remains debate about whether Sirhan acted alone, as the trajectory of bullets and witness accounts suggested a second shooter may have been involved.
lastSeen: June 4, 1968, Ambassador Hotel, Los Angeles, CA

name: Zodiac Killer Victims
summary: At least five confirmed victims were killed by an unidentified serial killer in Northern California between 1968 and 1969. The killer taunted police and newspapers with cryptic letters and ciphers signed with a crosshair symbol. Despite thousands of suspects being investigated over decades, the Zodiac was never identified or arrested.

name: Bryan Hartnell & Cecelia Shepard
summary: Bryan Hartnell and Cecelia Shepard were attacked by the Zodiac Killer at Lake Berryessa, Napa County. Both were stabbed multiple times; Cecelia died two days later. The Zodiac called the Napa police to report the crime and left a coded message on Bryan's car door. Hartnell survived and provided a description.

name: Charles Manson Victims
summary: Charles Manson directed his followers to commit a series of brutal murders in Los Angeles in August 1969, including actress Sharon Tate. Multiple cult members were convicted. Manson himself was convicted of conspiracy to commit murder in 1971. The killings shocked American society and ended the 1960s counterculture movement.

name: Indiana Dunes Jane Doe
summary: An unidentified young woman's remains were found in the Indiana Dunes area in July 1969. Despite forensic examination spanning decades, she has never been identified. Her case remains in NamUs.

name: Jane Britton
summary: Harvard graduate student Jane Britton was found bludgeoned to death in her Cambridge apartment. Red ochre was sprinkled on her body. The case went unsolved for 50 years until DNA evidence in 2018 identified biology professor Michael Sumpter, who had died in 2001. The case was officially closed without prosecution.
lastSeen: January 7, 1969, her apartment in Cambridge, MA

name: Mary Jo Kopechne
summary: Mary Jo Kopechne drowned when a car driven by Senator Ted Kennedy went off a bridge on Chappaquiddick Island. Kennedy survived and waited nine hours before reporting the accident. He pleaded guilty to leaving the scene. Many questions about what happened that night remain unanswered.

name: Nebraska Jane Doe (1969)
summary: Skeletal remains of an unidentified woman were discovered near Lincoln, Nebraska in June 1969. She was estimated to be young adult at time of death. Despite forensic examination, her identity has never been established and her case remains open.

name: Patricia Blough & Renée Bruder
summary: Patricia Blough and Renée Bruder were murdered on July 4, 1969 near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. They were with two male friends who survived. Despite investigation over decades, the killer was never identified.

name: Paul Stine
summary: Cab driver Paul Stine was shot in the back of the head by the Zodiac Killer while stopped in the Presidio Heights neighborhood of San Francisco. The Zodiac sent police a piece of Stine's bloodied shirt to prove his guilt. This was the last confirmed Zodiac killing, though the killer was never caught.
lastSeen: October 11, 1969, Presidio Heights, San Francisco, CA

name: Sharon Tate
summary: Actress Sharon Tate, eight months pregnant, was murdered with four others by Manson Family members at her home in Los Angeles. The killings were directed by Charles Manson, who was convicted along with his followers. The case shocked Hollywood and America, symbolizing the dark end of the 1960s.
lastSeen: August 8, 1969, her home at 10050 Cielo Drive, Los Angeles

name: Sharon Tate & LaBianca Murders
summary: Actress Sharon Tate and four others were murdered at the Polanski home in Bel Air. The next night, Leno and Rosemary LaBianca were killed. Charles Manson directed his followers Charles 'Tex' Watson, Susan Atkins, and Patricia Krenwinkel. All were convicted. The killings horrified America and ended the 1960s counterculture.
lastSeen: August 8, 1969, Cielo Drive, Bel Air, CA

name: Dean Corll Victims
summary: Dean Corll, with accomplices David Brooks and Elmer Wayne Henley, tortured and murdered at least 28 young men and boys in Houston. Corll was shot and killed by Henley in 1973, who then led police to multiple burial sites. Henley and Brooks were both convicted. The case was one of America's worst serial murder cases.

name: Ann Burgess
summary: Twenty-four-year-old Ann Burgess was found murdered in Omaha, Nebraska in October 1971. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Douglas County cold case files.

name: Christine Marie Eastin
summary: Nineteen-year-old Christine Marie Eastin disappeared from Hayward, California on January 18, 1971. She was last seen near her home. Despite FBI involvement, she was never found. Her case remains on the FBI's unsolved missing persons list.
lastSeen: January 18, 1971, near her home in Hayward, CA

name: D.B. Cooper
summary: A man calling himself Dan Cooper hijacked Northwest Orient Flight 305 in 1971, extorted $200,000 in ransom, and parachuted from the plane somewhere over the Pacific Northwest. He was never identified or found. A small bundle of decomposed bills was found along the Columbia River in 1980. It remains the only unsolved commercial airline hijacking in US history.
lastSeen: November 24, 1971, somewhere over the Pacific Northwest

name: D.B. Cooper Hijacking
summary: A man calling himself Dan Cooper hijacked a Northwest Orient Boeing 727, received $200,000 in ransom, and parachuted from the plane over the Pacific Northwest. He was never identified or found. A partial bundle of the ransom money was found in 1980. The FBI officially suspended active investigation in 2016 after 45 years.
lastSeen: November 24, 1971, somewhere over Pacific Northwest during flight

name: List Family Murders
summary: John List murdered his wife, three teenage children, and elderly mother in their Westfield, New Jersey mansion, then vanished for 18 years. He was profiled on America's Most Wanted, and a viewer recognized him from the clay facial recreation. He was found in Colorado in 1989 and convicted in 1990.
lastSeen: November 9, 1971, family home in Westfield, NJ

name: Robert Hansen Victims
summary: Robert Hansen was an Anchorage baker who abducted women, flew them to remote Alaska wilderness, and hunted them. He confessed to 17 murders and was convicted in 1984. He died in prison in 2014. Some of his victims were never found and his total number of murders remains uncertain.

name: Texas Killing Fields Victims
summary: At least 11 women and girls were found dead or disappeared in a stretch of wetlands outside League City, Texas known as the Killing Fields. Only some victims have been identified. Multiple suspects have been investigated but most murders remain unsolved. The area became notorious as one of Texas's darkest cold case clusters.

name: William Dean Christiansen
summary: A series of murders in the Houston Heights neighborhood were attributed to William Dean Christiansen. He was convicted of multiple counts and sentenced to life. His case represented one of Houston's early serial killer investigations.

name: Dorothy Hunt
summary: Dorothy Hunt, wife of Watergate figure E. Howard Hunt, was killed in a United Airlines plane crash near Chicago. She was allegedly carrying $10,000 in cash. Some investigators and family members believed the crash was sabotage, possibly due to Watergate-related knowledge. The official finding was an accident.
lastSeen: December 8, 1972, United Flight 553 near Chicago, IL

name: Frank Foss Family
summary: Frank Foss and his wife were found shot to death in their rural home in Alamance County, North Carolina. The crime shocked the local community and despite investigation, no arrests were ever made. The case remains in county cold case files.
lastSeen: September 26, 1972, family home in Alamance County, NC

name: Georgia Jane Doe (1972)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found in Fulton County, Georgia in October 1972. She has never been identified despite forensic examination. Her case is entered in NamUs.

name: John Wayne Gacy Victims
summary: John Wayne Gacy tortured and murdered 33 young men and boys, burying 26 under his house. He was convicted in 1980 and executed in 1994. After his execution, investigators used DNA to identify unknown victims. Some remain unidentified. He performed as 'Pogo the Clown' at charity events, earning him the 'Killer Clown' nickname.

name: Barbara Gibbons
summary: Barbara Gibbons was found murdered in her Canaan, Connecticut home. Her eighteen-year-old son Peter Reilly was initially convicted based on a coerced confession but later exonerated. The actual killer has never been identified. The case spurred Connecticut reforms in interrogation procedures.
lastSeen: September 28, 1973, her home in Canaan, CT

name: BTK Killer Victims
summary: Dennis Rader, known as BTK (Bind, Torture, Kill), murdered 10 people in and around Wichita, Kansas between 1974 and 1991. He taunted police and media for decades. After lying dormant, he began communicating again in 2004 and was caught when metadata from a floppy disk he sent led to his identification. He pleaded guilty in 2005.

name: Beverly Jean Smith
summary: Sixteen-year-old Beverly Jean Smith disappeared from Huntington, West Virginia in February 1974. Despite investigation, she was never found. Her case is in NamUs as an ongoing investigation.

name: Carol Ann Smith
summary: Twenty-two-year-old Carol Ann Smith was found murdered in Tulsa, Oklahoma in December 1974. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Tulsa County cold case files.

name: Coral Eugene Watts Victims
summary: Coral Eugene Watts confessed to 13 murders in Michigan and Texas. He was convicted of burglary with intent to murder in Texas in 1982 and given a 60-year sentence. When he was nearly released in 2004, prosecutors charged him with additional murders using cold case evidence. He died in prison in 2007.

name: Golden State Killer Victims
summary: The Golden State Killer committed at least 13 murders, 50 rapes, and 100 burglaries across California. Joseph James DeAngelo, a former police officer, was identified in 2018 through genealogy DNA testing and pleaded guilty in 2020. For decades the case was considered nearly unsolvable, making the DNA breakthrough revolutionary.

name: Helen Grayco
summary: Thirty-one-year-old Helen Grayco was found murdered in Santa Ana, California in September 1974. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Orange County cold case files.

name: Karen Silkwood
summary: Nuclear power plant worker Karen Silkwood died in a suspicious single-car accident while driving to meet a reporter with documents about safety violations at the Kerr-McGee plutonium plant. The documents were never found. Her case inspired a major film and raised questions about nuclear industry whistleblower protection.

name: Margaret Ellen Fox
summary: Fourteen-year-old Margaret Fox disappeared from Burlington, New Jersey in June 1974. She was last seen near her home. Despite investigation spanning decades, she was never found. Her case remains on the FBI's unsolved missing persons list.
lastSeen: June 24, 1974, near her home in Burlington, NJ

name: Otero Family Murders
summary: The Otero family — Joseph, Julie, Joseph Jr., and Josephine — were bound and murdered in their Wichita home by BTK killer Dennis Rader. This was BTK's first confirmed attack. Rader was not identified for 30 years. When he began communicating with police again in 2004, a floppy disk trace led to his identification and conviction.
lastSeen: January 15, 1974, family home in Wichita, KS

name: Patty Hearst Kidnapping
summary: Newspaper heiress Patty Hearst was abducted from her Berkeley apartment by the Symbionese Liberation Army. After months in captivity she participated in a bank robbery. She was convicted in 1976 but pardoned by President Carter and later given a full pardon by President Clinton.
lastSeen: February 4, 1974, her apartment in Berkeley, CA

name: Steven Weed
summary: Patricia Hearst, heiress to the Hearst media empire, was kidnapped by the Symbionese Liberation Army in Berkeley. She was held for months and famously participated in a bank robbery. This case relates to the SLA's murders and violent acts. Hearst was eventually convicted of bank robbery but pardoned in 2001.

name: Ted Bundy Victims
summary: Ted Bundy confessed to 30 homicides across seven states, though the actual number is believed higher. He was executed in Florida in 1989. His final crimes were committed at Florida State University, where he attacked sorority members. Bundy became one of the most notorious serial killers in American history.

name: Ted Bundy Washington Victims
summary: Several of Ted Bundy's earliest confirmed victims disappeared from Washington State in 1974, including Lynda Ann Healy. Bundy attended the University of Washington before his killing spree expanded across the country. Most Washington victims were never found.

name: University of New Mexico Murders (1974)
summary: Three female students were murdered near the University of New Mexico campus in 1974. Despite investigation and connection theories to other cases, the killer was never identified. The cases remain open with Albuquerque Police.

name: David Williams & Steven Anderson
summary: Twelve-year-old David Williams and seventeen-year-old Steven Anderson both disappeared from Pemberton Township, New Jersey on April 7, 1975. Neither was ever found. The dual disappearance mystified investigators and their cases remain among New Jersey's most puzzling cold cases.

name: Jimmy Hoffa
summary: Teamsters Union president Jimmy Hoffa was last seen in a restaurant parking lot in Bloomfield Township, Michigan. He was believed to have been murdered by organized crime figures. Multiple sites were searched over the years as locations of his remains. He was declared legally dead in 1982. His body was never found.
lastSeen: July 30, 1975, Machus Red Fox restaurant, Bloomfield Township, MI

name: Karen Ann Quinlan
summary: Karen Ann Quinlan fell into a persistent vegetative state after mixing alcohol and tranquilizers. Her parents won a landmark Supreme Court case to remove her ventilator. She survived without it for 9 years before dying in 1985. The circumstances leading to her coma were never fully explained.

name: Laura Woolheater
summary: Twenty-three-year-old Laura Woolheater was murdered in Portland, Oregon in December 1975. Daniel Cook was convicted of her murder in 2005 after cold case investigators matched DNA from the scene. The case remained cold for 30 years before advanced DNA testing cracked it open.

name: Myrna Opsahl
summary: Myrna Opsahl was shot and killed during a bank robbery by members of the Symbionese Liberation Army in Carmichael, California. Patricia Hearst and several SLA members were later convicted of the robbery in 2003 for charges related to the crime. The triggerman Emily Harris was also convicted.
lastSeen: April 21, 1975, Crocker National Bank, Carmichael, CA

name: Robert Lee Yates Victims
summary: Robert Lee Yates Jr. was a decorated Army National Guard helicopter pilot who confessed to 13 murders in eastern Washington and convicted of two in Oregon. He targeted mostly sex workers in Spokane. He was sentenced to 408 years and to death in California. He died in prison from natural causes.

name: South Dakota Jane Doe (1975)
summary: Skeletal remains of an unidentified woman were found near Rapid City, South Dakota in May 1975. She has never been identified despite forensic examination. Her case remains on file with South Dakota investigators.

name: Anna Mae Aquash
summary: Mi'kmaq activist Anna Mae Aquash was found shot dead on the Pine Ridge Reservation. She had been an American Indian Movement leader. Arlo Looking Cloud and John Graham were eventually convicted of her murder in 2004 and 2010, revealing internal AIM conflicts. The case highlighted political violence on reservations.

name: James Benecke (EAR/ONS)
summary: The East Area Rapist / Original Night Stalker committed at least 51 rapes and 12 murders in California. True crime writer Michelle McNamara coined the name Golden State Killer while investigating the case. Her book 'I'll Be Gone in the Dark' was published posthumously. Joseph DeAngelo was identified via genealogy DNA in 2018.

name: Kansas Turnpike Jane Doe
summary: An unidentified young woman's body was found near the Kansas Turnpike in October 1976. She was never identified despite forensic examination. Her case remains in NamUs as investigators continue trying to establish her identity.

name: Oakland County Child Killer Victims
summary: A serial killer known as the 'Babysitter' abducted and murdered four children in Oakland County, Michigan. The victims ranged in age from 10 to 12 years old. The killer washed and redressed victims before leaving them in plain sight. Despite one of the largest investigations in Michigan history, the killer was never identified.

name: Oklahoma Jane Doe (1976)
summary: Skeletal remains of an unidentified woman were found near Oklahoma City in April 1976. Despite forensic examination and recent DNA analysis, she has never been identified. Her case is entered in NamUs.

name: Son of Sam Victims
summary: David Berkowitz, known as 'Son of Sam,' murdered six people and wounded seven others in New York City over 13 months. He terrorized the city with taunting letters to police and media. He was caught in August 1977 after a parking ticket near the last murder scene. He pleaded guilty and is serving six consecutive 25-years-to-life sentences.

name: Wisconsin Dells Jane Doe
summary: The skeletal remains of an unidentified young woman were found in Wisconsin Dells in January 1976. Despite forensic analysis and DNA testing decades later, she has never been identified. Her case remains in NamUs.

name: Bonnie Garland
summary: Twenty-one-year-old Yale student Bonnie Garland was killed with a hammer by her Yale student boyfriend Richard Herrin while she slept at her parents' Scarsdale home. Herrin claimed he was in a dissociative state. He was convicted of manslaughter and served 15 years. The case raised debates about the psychology of violence.
lastSeen: July 16, 1977, parents' home in Scarsdale, NY

name: Dorothy Sherrill
summary: Dorothy Sherrill was found murdered in Cabarrus County, North Carolina in July 1977. The case went cold. It remains in Cabarrus County cold case files.

name: Hillside Strangler Victims
summary: Cousins Angelo Buono and Kenneth Bianchi posed as police officers, kidnapped, raped, and murdered 10 women in Los Angeles. Their victims were found on hillsides, giving rise to the 'Hillside Strangler' name. Both were convicted: Bianchi in 1979 and Buono in 1983.

name: Holly Maddux
summary: Holly Maddux disappeared in September 1977. Her mummified body was found in a trunk in her boyfriend Ira Einhorn's Philadelphia apartment in 1979. Einhorn, a prominent counterculture figure, fled to Europe before trial. He was convicted in absentia in 1993 and extradited from France in 2001, serving life in prison until his death in 2020.

name: Judy Ann Morley
summary: Nineteen-year-old Judy Ann Morley was found murdered in Gainesville, Florida in December 1977. Her case predates the Gainesville murders of 1990. Despite investigation, her killer was never identified. The case remains in Alachua County cold case files.

name: Roger Kibbe Victims
summary: Roger Kibbe was convicted of murdering seven women in California, known as the I-5 Strangler. His victims were found along freeways. A cold case detective reopened the cases in the early 2000s using DNA evidence, finally convicting Kibbe in 2009. He died in prison in 2021.

name: St. Louis Jane Doe (1977)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found in St. Louis, Missouri in August 1977. Despite forensic examination and DNA testing, she has never been identified. Her case remains in NamUs.

name: Amy Carney
summary: Twenty-three-year-old Amy Carney was found strangled in her Providence, Rhode Island apartment. Despite investigation, no arrests were made and the case went cold. It remains in Rhode Island State Police files.
lastSeen: February 3, 1978, her apartment in Providence, RI

name: Beverly McLean
summary: Thirty-six-year-old Beverly McLean was found murdered in Dallas, Texas in September 1978. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Dallas County cold case files.

name: Bob Crane
summary: TV actor Bob Crane, star of Hogan's Heroes, was found bludgeoned to death in his Scottsdale apartment. His acquaintance John Carpenter was tried for the murder in 1994 but acquitted. Despite physical evidence and suspicion falling on Carpenter, no one has ever been convicted of the crime.
lastSeen: June 28, 1978, Windmill Dinner Theatre, Scottsdale, AZ

name: FSU Chi Omega Murders
summary: Ted Bundy broke into the Chi Omega sorority at Florida State University, killing two women and injuring two others. He also attacked a student at another location that night. Bundy was identified via bite mark evidence and convicted in 1979. His execution in 1989 ended his killing spree.
lastSeen: January 15, 1978, Chi Omega House, FSU, Tallahassee, FL

name: Jeffrey Dahmer Victims
summary: Jeffrey Dahmer murdered 17 young men and boys in Wisconsin and Ohio, engaging in necrophilia and cannibalism. He was convicted of 15 murders in Wisconsin and sentenced to 15 life terms. Fellow inmate Christopher Scarver beat him to death in prison in 1994. His case became the subject of multiple documentaries and a Netflix series.

name: Judy Chartier
summary: Twenty-two-year-old Judy Chartier was found murdered in Waterbury, Connecticut in October 1978. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Connecticut State Police files.

name: Kimberly Leach
summary: Twelve-year-old Kimberly Leach was the last victim of Ted Bundy, abducted from her middle school in Lake City, Florida. Her remains were found in a pig shed. Bundy was convicted of her murder and sentenced to death, later executed in 1989 for her and other murders.
lastSeen: February 9, 1978, Lake City Junior High School, Lake City, FL

name: Norma Jean Aragon
summary: Sixteen-year-old Norma Jean Aragon was found murdered in New Haven, Connecticut in September 1978. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in New Haven Police cold case files.

name: Rep. Leo Ryan & Jonestown Victims
summary: U.S. Congressman Leo Ryan was shot dead at an airstrip in Guyana along with members of his delegation while investigating Jim Jones's Peoples Temple. Hours later, 918 Temple members died in Jonestown in the largest mass death in American history. Larry Layton was convicted of the Ryan murder and served 37 years.

name: Ann Hilley
summary: Frank Hilley died of a mysterious illness in Anniston, Alabama. His wife Marie was eventually convicted of murder by arsenic poisoning in 1983. Marie had also poisoned her daughter Carol, who survived. Marie Hilley escaped custody in 1987, created a new identity, and was recaptured and died in prison in 1987.
lastSeen: May 25, 1979, family home in Anniston, AL

name: Atlanta Child Murders Victims
summary: At least 28 young Black children and adults were murdered in Atlanta between 1979 and 1981, causing citywide panic. Wayne Williams was convicted of two adult murders and sentenced to life. Authorities attributed the child murders to him but never charged him with those. Investigations were reopened in 2019 amid questions about the evidence.

name: Brenda Casey
summary: Twenty-four-year-old Brenda Casey was found murdered in Knoxville, Tennessee in October 1979. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Knox County cold case files.

name: Bucks County Jane Doe
summary: An unidentified young woman's remains were found in Bucks County, Pennsylvania in 1979. Despite decades of investigation and modern DNA analysis, she has never been identified. The case remains in NamUs and investigators continue working to establish her identity.

name: Etan Patz
summary: Six-year-old Etan Patz was one of the first missing children to appear on a milk carton when he disappeared walking to school in SoHo. The case transformed child safety in America. Pedro Hernandez confessed to the murder in 2012 and was convicted in 2017, ending a 38-year mystery.
lastSeen: May 25, 1979, walking to school bus in SoHo, New York City

name: Kentucky Jane Doe (1979)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found near Louisville, Kentucky in September 1979. She has never been identified despite forensic examination. Her case remains open in Jefferson County files.

name: Linda Mack
summary: Thirty-two-year-old Linda Mack was found murdered in Green Bay, Wisconsin in February 1979. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Brown County cold case files.

name: Robin Elizabeth Voss
summary: Seventeen-year-old Robin Elizabeth Voss was found murdered in Milwaukee, Wisconsin in September 1979. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Milwaukee County cold case files.

name: Susan Reinert
summary: English teacher Susan Reinert was found dead in the trunk of her car in Ardmore with her two children missing and never found. Her colleague and former lover William Bradfield was convicted of her murder, and Dr. Jay C. Smith, a school principal, was later convicted of the three murders but his convictions were overturned. The children were never found.

name: Wayne Williams Conviction Review
summary: Wayne Williams was convicted in 1982 of two adult murders during the Atlanta Child Murders era. Atlanta police attributed most of the child deaths to him despite no direct evidence. In 2019, Atlanta reopened the investigation after DNA evidence was reexamined. The case remains partially solved.

name: Alaska Jane Doe (1980)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found near Fairbanks, Alaska in July 1980. She has never been identified despite forensic examination. Her case is entered in NamUs.

name: Betty Gore
summary: Betty Gore was found brutally axed to death in her home in Wylie, Texas. Her neighbor and friend, Candy Montgomery, was charged with the murder. Montgomery claimed self-defense and was acquitted in a famous trial. The case was dramatized in multiple TV series decades later.
lastSeen: June 13, 1980, her home in Wylie, TX

name: Dorothy Jane Scott
summary: Dorothy Jane Scott disappeared after driving a sick coworker to the hospital in Anaheim. Her burned car was found abandoned nearby. A stalker had been calling her at work making threats. Over the next four years, an unknown caller continued to contact her family claiming to have killed her. Her remains were found in 1984 but the killer was never identified.
lastSeen: May 28, 1980, Western Medical Center, Anaheim, CA

name: Herman Tarnower
summary: Dr. Herman Tarnower, creator of the Scarsdale Diet, was shot at his Purchase, New York home by his longtime companion Jean Harris. Harris claimed she intended to kill herself but shot him accidentally during a struggle. She was convicted of second-degree murder and sentenced to 15 years. Governor Cuomo commuted her sentence.
lastSeen: March 10, 1980, his home in Purchase, NY

name: Jeannie Mills Family
summary: Jeannie Mills, one of the early defectors from Jim Jones's Peoples Temple, was murdered along with her husband Al and their daughter Linda in their Berkeley home. She had been a vocal critic of Jones and Jonestown. The killer was never identified despite investigation.
lastSeen: February 26, 1980, family home in Berkeley, CA

name: Oregon Jane Doe (1980)
summary: Skeletal remains of an unidentified woman were found in Deschutes County, Oregon in April 1980. Despite forensic examination and DNA testing, she has never been identified. Her case is entered in NamUs.

name: Tammy Terrell
summary: An unidentified 17-year-old woman was found dead in Henderson, Nevada in May 1980. She may have been a victim of human trafficking. Despite forensic examination, she was never identified. Her case remains in Clark County cold case files.

name: Adam Walsh
summary: Six-year-old Adam Walsh was abducted from a Sears store in Hollywood, Florida. His severed head was found two weeks later. The case transformed his father John Walsh into a victims' rights advocate who created America's Most Wanted. Otis Toole confessed to the murder but died in prison; he was officially named as the killer in 2008.

name: Dean and Tina Clouse
summary: The decomposing remains of Dean and Tina Clouse were found in Houston, Texas in 1981. They had reportedly been associated with a 'Jesus freak' cult. Their infant daughter Lisa was found alive having been abandoned. Cult members were investigated but never charged.

name: Janet Leigh Wilson
summary: Nineteen-year-old Janet Leigh Wilson was murdered in Wichita, Kansas in April 1981. Her case has been investigated by Wichita Police but remains unsolved. Cold case investigators have reviewed the evidence in recent years.

name: Keddie Cabin Murders
summary: Glenna Sharp and her son Johnny and family friend Dana Wingate were bound, stabbed, and bludgeoned in Cabin 28 at Keddie resort. The other Sharp children survived by hiding in an adjacent cabin. A skull found years later was identified as the fourth victim. The case was only partially solved; Martin Smartt and John Boubede were suspects who both died before charges could be filed.

name: Marcia King (Buckskin Girl)
summary: Known as 'Buckskin Girl' for the fringed jacket she wore, Marcia King was found murdered along US Route 40 near Troy, Ohio in April 1981. She was unidentified for 37 years until DNA genealogy testing identified her in 2018. Her killer has never been found.

name: Moorhead Jane Doe
summary: An unidentified young woman's remains were found near Moorhead, Minnesota in March 1981. She was found in a wooded area. Despite forensic testing and DNA analysis in recent years, she has never been identified.

name: Pamela LePore
summary: Twenty-year-old Pamela LePore was found murdered in Manchester, New Hampshire. Her case went cold for decades despite investigation. The New Hampshire State Police cold case unit has continued to work the case.

name: Philip Testa
summary: Philadelphia mob boss Philip 'Chicken Man' Testa was killed by a nail bomb planted under his front porch. The bombing sparked a Mafia war within the Philadelphia crime family. Despite the Mafia connection, no one was formally charged with planting the bomb.
lastSeen: March 15, 1981, his home in Philadelphia, PA

name: Sebastian Russo
summary: Physician Sebastian Russo was shot in his Baltimore office, apparently targeted for prescription drugs. Despite investigation, no suspects were identified or charged. The case remains open in Baltimore Police cold case files.
lastSeen: April 1981, medical office in Baltimore, MD

name: Tammy Mahoney
summary: Nineteen-year-old Tammy Mahoney disappeared from Oneida, New York on May 8, 1981. She was last seen leaving her boyfriend's home. Despite decades of investigation and FBI involvement, Tammy has never been found and no suspects have been charged.
lastSeen: May 8, 1981, leaving her boyfriend's home in Oneida, NY

name: Chicago Jane Doe (1982)
summary: The remains of an unidentified woman were found in a wooded area in Cook County, Illinois in October 1982. Despite forensic examination and DNA testing, she has never been identified. Her case is entered in NamUs and investigators continue to seek answers regarding her identity.

name: Chicago Tylenol Murders Victims
summary: Seven people in Chicago died after taking cyanide-laced Tylenol capsules in 1982. The deaths prompted a nationwide recall and led to tamper-resistant packaging regulations. James W. Lewis was convicted of extortion related to the case but was never charged with the murders. The killer has never been identified.

name: Diane Suzuki
summary: Twenty-one-year-old Diane Suzuki was found strangled in Honolulu, Hawaii. Despite investigation by Honolulu Police Department, her killer was never identified or arrested. The case remains open in Hawaii cold case files.

name: Dorothy Puente Victims
summary: Dorothy Puente was a Sacramento boardinghouse operator who poisoned elderly tenants and continued cashing their Social Security checks. Seven bodies were found buried in her yard in 1988. She was convicted of three murders and died in prison in 2011. She remains one of history's most notorious female serial killers.

name: Gary Ridgway Unidentified Victims
summary: Beyond his confirmed 49 victims, Gary Ridgway claimed to have killed as many as 90 women. Several of the confirmed victims were not identified for years. Ridgway continues to provide information from prison about additional victims and burial sites he remembers.

name: Green River Killer Victims
summary: Gary Ridgway, the Green River Killer, murdered at least 49 women in Washington State. He targeted mostly sex workers and runaways. Ridgway was convicted in 2003 after DNA evidence linked him to four victims. He pleaded guilty and led investigators to several bodies in exchange for avoiding the death penalty.

name: Johnny Gosch
summary: Twelve-year-old Johnny Gosch disappeared while delivering newspapers in West Des Moines, Iowa. He was the first person to appear on a milk carton under the new program. His mother Noreen Gosch became a fierce advocate for missing children. In 1997, a young man claiming to be Johnny visited her but his identity was never confirmed.
lastSeen: September 5, 1982, on his paper route in West Des Moines, IA

name: Kathleen McCormack Durst
summary: Kathleen McCormack Durst, wife of real estate heir Robert Durst, disappeared from their South Salem home. Robert Durst was investigated but not charged. He later killed a neighbor in Texas and was acquitted of that murder. He was eventually convicted of killing a close friend Susan Berman who knew about Kathleen's fate. He died in prison in 2022.
lastSeen: January 31, 1982, family home in South Salem, NY

name: Kellie Poppleton
summary: Seventeen-year-old Kellie Poppleton disappeared from Omaha, Nebraska in July 1982 and was found murdered. Her killer has never been identified or charged. The case remains in Nebraska State Patrol cold case files.

name: Las Vegas Jane Doe (1982)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found in the desert outside Las Vegas in August 1982. She has never been identified. The case is entered in NamUs and Clark County investigators continue to seek her identity.

name: Patricia Meehan
summary: Twenty-eight-year-old Patricia Meehan was found murdered in Camden, New Jersey in April 1982. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Camden County cold case files.

name: Tahoe Jane Doe
summary: The skeletal remains of an unidentified young woman were found on the shores of Lake Tahoe in May 1982. Despite decades of investigation and forensic testing including DNA, her identity has never been established. She is listed in NamUs and the case remains open.

name: Helen Sims
summary: Forty-one-year-old Helen Sims was found murdered in Macon, Georgia in July 1983. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Bibb County cold case files.

name: Margaret Kilcoyne
summary: Thirty-five-year-old Margaret Kilcoyne was found murdered in Waltham, Massachusetts in October 1983. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Middlesex County cold case files.

name: Matthew Falkner
summary: A homicide victim found in Albany, New York in January 1983 remains unidentified and unsolved. Despite investigation by Albany County investigators, the case went cold and remains in their files.

name: Stephanie Coffey
summary: Twenty-year-old Ohio University student Stephanie Coffey was found murdered near the campus in Athens, Ohio. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Athens County cold case files.
lastSeen: September 9, 1983, Ohio University campus area, Athens, OH

name: Wah Mee Club Massacre
summary: Thirteen people were robbed and murdered at the Wah Mee private gambling club in Seattle's Chinatown. Ng Wai-chiu, Tony Ng, and Willie Mak committed the massacre. Mak and Ng Wai-chiu received death sentences; Tony Ng received 13 life sentences. It remains the largest mass murder in Washington State history.
lastSeen: February 19, 1983, Wah Mee Club, Seattle, WA

name: West Virginia Jane Doe (1983)
summary: Skeletal remains of an unidentified young woman were found near Morgantown, West Virginia in June 1983. Despite forensic examination and DNA testing, she has never been identified. Her case remains in NamUs.

name: Angela Jane Stokes
summary: Twenty-three-year-old Angela Jane Stokes was found murdered in Jacksonville, Florida in November 1984. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Duval County cold case files.

name: Angela Samota
summary: Twenty-year-old Southern Methodist University student Angela Samota was sexually assaulted and murdered in her Dallas apartment. The case went cold for more than 20 years before a DNA database match linked Ricky Nolen Davis to the crime. Davis was convicted in 2009, 25 years after the murder.
lastSeen: September 27, 1984, her apartment in Dallas, TX

name: Night Stalker Victims
summary: Richard Ramirez, the Night Stalker, terrorized Southern California committing 14 murders, rapes, and burglaries. He was captured after residents of Mission Viejo recognized him and held him until police arrived. He was convicted on 43 counts and sentenced to death. He died of cancer on death row in 2013.

name: Vicki Lynne Hoskinson
summary: Eight-year-old Vicki Lynne Hoskinson disappeared while mailing a letter near her Tucson home. Her remains were found eight months later. Frank Jarvis Atwood was convicted in 1987 and spent decades on death row, becoming a cause célèbre for death penalty opponents. He was executed in 2022.
lastSeen: September 17, 1984, near her home in Tucson, AZ

name: Ann McLaughlin
summary: Thirty-two-year-old Ann McLaughlin was found murdered in Bedford, New Hampshire in September 1985. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Hillsborough County cold case files.

name: Deborah Dudley
summary: An unsolved homicide from Portland, Maine in November 1985 remains in cold case files with Maine State Police. Despite investigation, no arrests were made in this case.

name: Grim Sleeper Victims
summary: Lonnie Franklin Jr., known as the Grim Sleeper, was convicted of killing 10 women in Los Angeles between 1985 and 2007. He appeared to stop killing for years (hence 'Grim Sleeper') and was identified through familial DNA from his son. He was convicted in 2016 and died on death row in 2020.

name: Nancy Leichner
summary: Twenty-year-old Nancy Leichner disappeared from Nashville, Tennessee in July 1985. She was a student at Vanderbilt University. Despite investigation, she was never found. Her case remains in Tennessee Bureau of Investigation files.

name: Robeson County Jane Doe (1985)
summary: An unidentified young woman's remains were found in Robeson County, North Carolina in August 1985. She has never been identified despite forensic examination and DNA testing. The case remains in NamUs.

name: Shari Smith
summary: Seventeen-year-old Shari Smith was abducted and killed in Saluda County, South Carolina. Larry Gene Bell kidnapped and killed Shari and later a nine-year-old girl, Debra May Helmick. Bell was convicted and executed in 1996. He taunted the Smith family with phone calls and letters.
lastSeen: May 31, 1985, family home in Saluda County, SC

name: Steve Christensen & Kathy Sheets
summary: Two people were killed by mail bombs in Salt Lake City — Steve Christensen at his office and Kathy Sheets at her home. The next day, the bomber Mark Hofmann was seriously injured when one of his own bombs exploded. Hofmann, a Mormon documents forger, had planted the bombs to distract from fraud investigations. He pleaded guilty and was sentenced to life.

name: Anthonette Christine Cayedito
summary: Nine-year-old Anthonette answered a knock at the door of her family home around 3 AM and was abducted. Her younger sister witnessed two men grab Anthonette and carry her away. A year later, a girl called 911 saying 'I'm Anthonette' before the line went dead. She has never been found.
lastSeen: April 6, 1986, family home in Gallup, NM

name: Cheryl Ferreira
summary: Twenty-four-year-old Cheryl Ferreira was found murdered in Cranston, Rhode Island in April 1986. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Providence County cold case files.

name: Colonial Parkway Murders
summary: Three couples and one woman were murdered along the Colonial Parkway in Virginia between 1986 and 1989. Despite being investigated by the FBI and state police, no one was ever charged. The killer or killers have never been identified, and the cases are considered among Virginia's most haunting cold cases.
lastSeen: Various dates along Colonial Pkwy, VA

name: Dorothy Miller
summary: Dorothy Miller was one of Dorothy Puente's victims at the Sacramento boarding house. Her death was among those discovered when seven bodies were found buried in Puente's yard in 1988. Puente's conviction covered three murders with the remaining deaths treated as circumstantial.

name: Gary Heidnik Victims
summary: Gary Heidnik kidnapped, tortured, and killed women in the basement of his Philadelphia home. He was arrested in 1987 when a victim escaped. Two women died. Heidnik was convicted of first-degree murder and executed in 1999. His case partly inspired the Thomas Harris character Buffalo Bill in 'Silence of the Lambs.'

name: Helen Taylor
summary: Twenty-five-year-old Helen Taylor was found murdered in Spokane, Washington in June 1986. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Spokane County cold case files.

name: Jennifer Levin
summary: Jennifer Levin was found strangled in Central Park near the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Her date, Robert Chambers, was suspected in what the tabloids called the 'Preppy Murder.' Chambers pleaded guilty to manslaughter in 1988 just before the jury returned. He was later imprisoned for drug charges.
lastSeen: August 26, 1986, Dorrian's Red Hand bar, Upper East Side, NYC

name: Josefina Rivera
summary: Josefina Rivera was one of six women abducted by Gary Heidnik and held in his basement in Philadelphia. Two women died. Rivera eventually helped Heidnik pick up victims and was later freed. She cooperated with police and Heidnik was convicted. Her case exemplified survival and cooperation under extreme duress.

name: Kimberly Dietz
summary: Nineteen-year-old Kimberly Dietz was found murdered in Buffalo, New York in August 1986. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Erie County cold case files.

name: Vermont Jane Doe (1986)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found in Burlington, Vermont in July 1986. She has never been identified despite forensic examination. Her case is entered in NamUs.

name: Connie Foster
summary: Twenty-six-year-old Connie Foster disappeared from Memphis, Tennessee in April 1987. Despite investigation, she was never found. Her case remains open with Memphis Police.

name: Lisa Steinberg
summary: Six-year-old Lisa Steinberg was beaten to death by Joel Steinberg, her illegal adoptive father, in their Greenwich Village apartment. Her abuse had been ongoing. Hedda Nussbaum, Steinberg's battered partner who was present during the beating, was granted immunity for her testimony. Steinberg was convicted of manslaughter.
lastSeen: November 2, 1987, apartment in Greenwich Village, NYC

name: Melinda Lou Reynolds
summary: Twenty-two-year-old Melinda Lou Reynolds was found murdered near Elizabethtown, Kentucky. Despite investigation by Kentucky State Police, the case went cold. Her family has continued to advocate for answers.

name: Peggy Hettrick
summary: Peggy Hettrick was found mutilated in a field in Fort Collins. Tim Masters was wrongfully convicted in 1999 and served nine years before his conviction was vacated in 2008 using new DNA and forensic evidence. The actual killer has never been identified.

name: Amber Swartz
summary: Seven-year-old Amber Swartz disappeared from near her Pinole, California home in June 1988. Despite massive searches and investigation, she was never found. Her case was one of several that motivated California child safety legislation.
lastSeen: June 3, 1988, near her home in Pinole, CA

name: Carolyn Short
summary: Thirty-year-old Carolyn Short was found murdered in Topeka, Kansas in March 1988. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Shawnee County cold case files.

name: Delaware Jane Doe (1988)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found in New Castle County, Delaware in June 1988. Despite forensic examination, she has never been identified. Her case remains in NamUs.

name: Donna Lee Norris
summary: Seventeen-year-old Donna Lee Norris disappeared from Lewiston, Maine in August 1988. She was last seen leaving a mall. Despite investigation, she was never found. Her case remains open.

name: Gary Holt
summary: Twenty-nine-year-old Gary Holt was murdered in St. Louis, Missouri in September 1988. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in St. Louis Metropolitan Police cold case files.

name: I-40 Corridor Victims
summary: Multiple female victims were found murdered along Interstate 40 through New Mexico and Texas over a period of years. Investigators believed a long-haul trucker was responsible but no suspect was ever identified or charged.

name: Michael Grant
summary: Twenty-six-year-old Michael Grant was found murdered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania in October 1988. Despite investigation by Pittsburgh Police, the case went cold. It remains in Allegheny County cold case files.

name: Tara Leigh Calico
summary: Nineteen-year-old Tara Calico went for a bike ride along Highway 47 and never returned. Her bicycle was never found. A year later, a polaroid photo of a bound young woman and boy was found at a Florida convenience store, possibly depicting Tara, but this was never confirmed. The case remains one of New Mexico's most enduring mysteries.
lastSeen: September 20, 1988, biking on Highway 47, Valencia County, NM

name: Aileen Wuornos Victims
summary: Aileen Wuornos killed seven men along Florida highways, claiming self-defense. She was convicted of six murders and executed in 2002. She became one of America's most studied female serial killers, with multiple books and the Oscar-winning film 'Monster' depicting her story.

name: Dan Broderick
summary: Betty Broderick shot and killed her ex-husband attorney Dan Broderick and his new wife Linda in La Jolla. The divorce had been bitter and acrimonious. Betty was convicted and sentenced to 32 years to life. The case became famous through TV movies and a Netflix series.
lastSeen: November 5, 1989, their home in La Jolla, CA

name: Danny Rolling Confession
summary: Before the Gainesville murders, Danny Rolling murdered three members of the Grissom family in Shreveport, Louisiana in 1989. He confessed to these murders as well. He was eventually executed in 2006 for the Florida murders.

name: Donna Freese
summary: Thirty-one-year-old Donna Freese was found murdered in Davenport, Iowa in October 1989. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Scott County cold case files.

name: Ilene Beth Misheloff
summary: Thirteen-year-old Ilene Misheloff disappeared while walking home from school in Dublin, California. She has never been found. A former neighbor was considered a suspect decades later. The FBI lists her case among the most important unsolved missing children cases.
lastSeen: January 30, 1989, walking home from Wells Middle School, Dublin, CA

name: Jacob Wetterling
summary: Eleven-year-old Jacob Wetterling was abducted at gunpoint near his home in St. Joseph, Minnesota. His disappearance led directly to the Jacob Wetterling Crimes Against Children and Sexually Violent Offender Registration Act. Danny Heinrich confessed to Jacob's abduction and murder in 2016, leading investigators to his remains after 27 years.
lastSeen: October 22, 1989, near his home in St. Joseph, MN

name: Lisa Bianco
summary: Lisa Bianco was killed by her abusive ex-husband Alan Matheney in Mishawaka, Indiana. Matheney was on a prison work-release program when he killed her. He was convicted but the case sparked significant reform of Indiana's work-release program and led to changes in how domestic violence victims are protected.
lastSeen: March 4, 1989, her home in Mishawaka, IN

name: Michelle Lee Sharp
summary: Twenty-two-year-old University of Virginia student Michelle Lee Sharp was found murdered in Charlottesville. Despite investigation by UVA and Charlottesville police, her killer was never identified. The case remains in Charlottesville cold case files.
lastSeen: February 26, 1989, UVA campus area, Charlottesville, VA

name: Patricia O'Donnell
summary: Thirty-three-year-old Patricia O'Donnell was found murdered in Freeport, New York. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Nassau County Police Department cold case files.

name: Ricky Brown (Central Park Five)
summary: The Central Park Jogger case involved the brutal rape and near-fatal beating of Trisha Meili in Central Park. Five teenagers — the Central Park Five — were wrongfully convicted and served years in prison. They were exonerated in 2002 when DNA evidence and a serial rapist's confession cleared them. The actual perpetrator, Matias Reyes, confessed but was not additionally charged.
lastSeen: April 19, 1989, Central Park, New York City

name: Robert Ben Rhoades Victims
summary: Robert Ben Rhoades was a long-haul trucker convicted of murdering a couple in 1990. He later confessed to 50 murders along US highways over 15 years. He was convicted of two murders and sentenced to life in Illinois. Many of his victims may never be identified.

name: Diana Olmstead
summary: Twenty-eight-year-old Diana Olmstead was found murdered in Ann Arbor, Michigan in August 1990. Despite investigation by Ann Arbor Police, the case went cold. It remains in Washtenaw County cold case files.

name: Gainesville Ripper Victims
summary: Danny Rolling murdered five University of Florida students in three days in Gainesville, Florida. He was sentenced to death and executed in 2006. The murders inspired the film Scream. Rolling's method of killing and posing victims caused mass fear across the campus.

name: Jacqueline Martarella
summary: Nineteen-year-old SUNY Stony Brook student Jacqueline Martarella was found murdered in June 1990. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Suffolk County cold case files.

name: Jan Marie Brophy
summary: Twenty-seven-year-old Jan Marie Brophy was found murdered in Flint, Michigan in August 1990. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Genesee County cold case files.

name: Kathleen Holland
summary: Boston College student Kathleen Holland was found murdered in her Boston apartment in April 1990. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Suffolk County cold case files.
lastSeen: April 4, 1990, her apartment in Boston, MA

name: Paige Renkoski
summary: Twenty-one-year-old Paige Renkoski disappeared while driving near Okemos, Michigan. Her car was found running with her purse inside. A witness reported seeing her talking to a man in another car. Despite investigation, she was never found. Her case remains in Ingham County Sheriff's Office files.

name: Terri Schiavo
summary: Terri Schiavo collapsed from cardiac arrest in St. Petersburg, Florida and suffered severe brain damage. She remained in a vegetative state for 15 years at the center of a national right-to-die legal battle. Her husband wanted to remove life support; her parents fought to keep it. Questions about the cause of her initial collapse were never fully resolved.
lastSeen: February 25, 1990, family home in St. Petersburg, FL

name: Terri Taber
summary: Nineteen-year-old Terri Taber was found murdered in her apartment in Elk River, Minnesota. She had been strangled. Despite investigation and a reward offered, no arrests have been made.
lastSeen: June 4, 1990, her apartment in Elk River, MN

name: Charles Albright Victims
summary: Charles Albright was convicted of one murder in Dallas but suspected in two additional cases. His victims' eyes were always removed post-mortem, earning him the nickname 'The Eyeball Killer.' He was sentenced to life imprisonment. Only one conviction was obtained despite three suspected murders.

name: Danny Casolaro
summary: Investigative journalist Danny Casolaro was found dead in a Martinsburg, West Virginia hotel bathtub. His death was ruled suicide. He had been investigating what he called 'The Octopus,' a vast government conspiracy involving BCCI, the Inslaw case, and Iran-Contra. His research notes were missing from his room.

name: Gary Dell-Brown
summary: Twenty-eight-year-old Gary Dell-Brown disappeared from Tulsa, Oklahoma in May 1991. Despite investigation by Tulsa Police, he was never found. His case remains open.

name: Latasha Harlins
summary: Fifteen-year-old Latasha Harlins was shot and killed by Korean-American store owner Soon Ja Du in Los Angeles. Du was convicted of voluntary manslaughter but given probation and no jail time. The lenient sentence inflamed racial tensions and contributed to the 1992 LA riots.
lastSeen: March 16, 1991, Empire Liquor Market, Los Angeles, CA

name: Linda Jane Sullivan
summary: Linda Jane Sullivan was found murdered in Tucson, Arizona in July 1991. Despite investigation by Tucson Police, the case went cold. It remains in Pima County Sheriff's Office cold case files.

name: Lois Teufen

name: Maine Jane Doe (1991)

name: Mark Himebaugh
summary: Eleven-year-old Mark Himebaugh disappeared from Del Haven, New Jersey on November 25, 1991. He was playing outside when he vanished. Despite extensive searches and FBI involvement, Mark has never been found.
lastSeen: November 25, 1991, near his home in Del Haven, NJ

name: Rachel Lopez
summary: Twenty-three-year-old Rachel Lopez was found murdered in Allentown, Pennsylvania in December 1991. Despite investigation by Allentown Police, the case went cold. It remains in Lehigh County cold case files.

name: Rodney King Beating
summary: Rodney King was beaten by LAPD officers after a high-speed chase. The beating was videotaped. Four officers were acquitted, sparking the 1992 LA riots. Federal charges led to two officers being convicted. King died in 2012 in a suspicious drowning. His death was ruled accidental.
lastSeen: March 3, 1991, Foothill Freeway, Los Angeles, CA

name: Sharon Sorkin
summary: Forty-seven-year-old Sharon Sorkin was found murdered in Boca Raton, Florida in September 1991. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Palm Beach County cold case files.

name: Stacy Richmond
summary: Twenty-two-year-old Stacy Richmond was found murdered in Kalamazoo, Michigan. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains open in Kalamazoo Public Safety cold case files.

name: Aaron Rains
summary: Twelve-year-old Aaron Rains disappeared from Youngstown, Ohio in July 1992. Despite searches, he was never found. His case remains open with Mahoning County investigators.
lastSeen: July 22, 1992, his neighborhood in Youngstown, OH

name: Christopher McCandless
summary: Christopher McCandless died in an abandoned bus in the Alaskan wilderness after venturing into the wild with minimal supplies. The cause of death was officially starvation, though some theories suggest poisoning from seeds. His story was told in the book and film 'Into the Wild.' The bus was removed from its location in 2020.
lastSeen: April 1992, entering Alaskan wilderness via Stampede Trail

name: I-70 Killer Victims
summary: Six employees of shoe and sporting goods stores were shot in April-May 1992 along Interstate 70 in Kansas, Missouri, and Indiana. All victims were shot from close range in the back of the head. A composite sketch was released but the killer was never identified.

name: James O'Dell
summary: Thirty-six-year-old James O'Dell was found murdered in Roanoke, Virginia in February 1992. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Roanoke City cold case files.

name: Janet Anderson

name: Jeremy Guillory

Langley confessed but has been tried three times due to procedural issues, receiving varying sentences. The case became the subject of a book and documentary, and remains legally complex.

name: Kimberly Kling
summary: Eight-year-old Kimberly Kling disappeared from her front yard in Palmerton, Pennsylvania. Her remains were found nearby two weeks later. Despite extensive investigation and numerous suspects, no one has been charged with her abduction and murder.
lastSeen: June 18, 1992, her front yard in Palmerton, PA

name: Phoenix Jane Doe (1992)
summary: An unidentified woman's remains were found in the desert east of Phoenix, Arizona in October 1992. She has never been identified despite forensic examination. The case is in NamUs and Maricopa County investigators continue working it.

name: Springfield Three
summary: Sherrill Levitt, her daughter Suzanne Streeter, and Suzanne's friend Stacy McCall all vanished from the Levitt home overnight. Their cars were still in the driveway, their purses inside. The porch light's glass globe was broken. Despite thousands of leads over 30 years, no trace of the three women has ever been found.
lastSeen: June 7, 1992, 1717 E. Delmar St., Springfield, MO

name: Tammy Zywicki
summary: College student Tammy Zywicki's car was found abandoned on Interstate 80 with a flat tire. Nine days later, her body was found wrapped in a sleeping bag along Interstate 44 in Missouri, over 500 miles away. A witness saw a semi-truck near her car. The trucker was never identified despite a nationwide search.

name: Holly Piirainen
summary: Ten-year-old Holly Piirainen disappeared while visiting grandparents in Sturbridge, Massachusetts. Her skeleton was found in a wooded area in 1993. A shoe of hers was found near where a man named Robert Sousa lived. Sousa was convicted of her murder in 2011 after more than 17 years as an open cold case.

name: Laquanda Benson

lastSeen: December 20, 1993, her apartment in Iowa City, IA

name: Mark Johnson
summary: Mark Johnson was found shot to death in Missoula, Montana in September 1993. Despite investigation, the case went cold and remains unsolved. His family has continued to seek answers for decades.

name: Polly Klaas
summary: Twelve-year-old Polly Klaas was kidnapped at knifepoint from a slumber party at her Petaluma home and murdered. Richard Allen Davis was convicted and sentenced to death. Polly's case led directly to California's 'three strikes' law and influenced child safety legislation nationwide.
lastSeen: October 1, 1993, her home in Petaluma, CA

name: Stacy Barker

lastSeen: January 29, 1993, leaving work in Atlanta, GA

summary: Three eight-year-old boys were murdered in West Memphis, Arkansas. Three teenagers — Damien Echols, Jason Baldwin, and Jessie Misskelley Jr. — were convicted but released in 2011 via Alford pleas. Controversy remains about their guilt. DNA from the crime scene has not been matched to anyone and some evidence pointed to a parent of one of the victims.

name: Teresa Parker
summary: Thirty-one-year-old Teresa Parker was found murdered in Savannah, Georgia in March 1993. Despite investigation, the case went cold. It remains in Chatham County cold case files.

name: Wendy Jerome
